title: call()
tldr: call() is a method on all functions that calls the function with a specified this value and arguments provided individually (comma-separated).
It allows you to borrow methods from one object and use them on another, and to explicitly control what this refers to.
Syntax: fn.call(thisArg, arg1, arg2, ...) — the function is invoked immediately.
call() is similar to apply(), but apply() takes arguments as an array.
laymanDefinition: Imagine you have a friend who is really good at fixing bikes. You don't know how to fix your own bike. With call(), it's like saying, 'Hey, bike-fixer friend, come over to my house and use my tools to fix my bike.' Your friend (the function) has a skill (the code), but you're saying 'Do it on my bike (this object) instead of yours.' More practically, call() lets you take a method from one object and run it 'as if' it belonged to a different object, passing in the arguments one by one.

deepDive.heading: How call() Works Internally
deepDive.text: When fn.call(thisArg, ...args) is called, the JavaScript engine internally performs a [[Call]] operation on fn. The thisArg becomes the 'this' value for the function execution. The remaining arguments are spread into the function's parameter list. If thisArg is null or undefined, 'this' defaults to the global object (or undefined in strict mode). If thisArg is a primitive, it is wrapped in its object wrapper (e.g., a string becomes a String object).

deepDive.heading: Method Borrowing Pattern
deepDive.text: One of the most powerful uses of call() is borrowing methods. For example, array methods can be called on array-like objects (like the arguments object, NodeLists, or any object with length and numeric properties). Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments, 0) converts the arguments object to a real array. Similarly, Object.prototype.toString.call(obj) gives the reliable [[Class]] tag like '[object Array]'.

deepDive.heading: call() vs Direct Invocation
deepDive.text: A direct function call fn() is equivalent to fn.call(undefined) in non-strict mode or fn.call(undefined) in strict mode (where 'this' is actually undefined). The key difference is that call() gives you explicit control over 'this'. Without call(), you rely on implicit binding (how the function is called), which may not always give you the desired 'this'.

deepDive.heading: Primitive thisArg Boxing
deepDive.text: When you pass a primitive value as thisArg to call(), JavaScript wraps it in the corresponding object type. For example, call('hello') boxes the string to a String object. This is why you can borrow methods that expect object 'this' values even when you only have primitives. However, in strict mode, primitives are not boxed — they are passed as-is, which may cause TypeErrors.

deepDive.heading: call() for Inheritance (Super Calls)
deepDive.text: In constructor functions, call() is used to call the parent constructor with the current instance's 'this'. This enables constructor stealing: Parent.call(this, ...args) inside Child constructor sets up Parent's properties on the Child instance. This pattern was common before ES6 classes, where super() handles this automatically.

interviewAnswer: call() is a function method that immediately invokes the function with a specified 'this' value and arguments passed individually. Its primary use cases are: 1) Explicitly setting 'this' — overriding the default binding rules. 2) Method borrowing — using methods from one object on another object (e.g., Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments)). 3) Constructor chaining — calling a parent constructor from a child constructor. 4) Reliable type checking — Object.prototype.toString.call(val) for precise type detection. The key characteristic of call() is that arguments are passed as a comma-separated list (unlike apply() which uses an array). It's essential to understand call() for mastering 'this' binding, method borrowing, and working with array-like objects.

interviewQuestions.question: What does call() do?
interviewQuestions.answer: call() invokes a function with a given 'this' value and arguments passed individually (comma-separated). Syntax: fn.call(thisArg, arg1, arg2, ...). It's used for explicit 'this' binding and method borrowing.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between call() and apply()?
interviewQuestions.answer: Both call() and apply() invoke a function immediately with a specified 'this'. The difference is in how arguments are passed: call() takes arguments as a comma-separated list (fn.call(obj, 1, 2, 3)), while apply() takes arguments as an array (fn.apply(obj, [1, 2, 3])).

interviewQuestions.question: How do you convert the arguments object to an array using call()?
interviewQuestions.answer: Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments) converts the array-like 'arguments' object into a real array. The arguments object has numeric indices and a length property, so slice() can operate on it. In modern JavaScript, Array.from() or the spread operator (...arguments) are preferred.

interviewQuestions.question: What is method borrowing? Give an example.
How do you use call() for constructor chaining?
What happens if you pass null or undefined as the thisArg to call()?
interviewQuestions.answer: In non-strict mode, null or undefined causes 'this' to default to the global object (window in browsers). In strict mode, 'this' remains null or undefined — it is not coerced to the global object.

interviewQuestions.question: How can call() be used for reliable type checking?
interviewQuestions.answer: Object.prototype.toString.call(value) returns a string like '[object Array]', '[object Object]', '[object Function]', etc. This is more reliable than typeof because it distinguishes between arrays, objects, and null. For example, Object.prototype.toString.call([]) returns '[object Array]'.

interviewQuestions.question: Can call() be used on methods that don't exist on the target object?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes. call() simply invokes a function with a specified 'this'. The function doesn't need to be a method of the target object. The target object is used as 'this' inside the function body. If the function accesses properties that don't exist on the target, they will be undefined.

interviewQuestions.question: How does call() handle primitive thisArg values?
interviewQuestions.answer: In non-strict mode, primitive values (string, number, boolean) are 'boxed' — converted to their object wrappers (String, Number, Boolean). In strict mode, primitives are used as-is. For example, fn.call('hello') passes a String object as 'this' in non-strict mode, but the primitive string 'hello' in strict mode.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the performance implication of using call()?
interviewQuestions.answer: call() has a small performance cost compared to direct invocation because it involves additional argument handling and 'this' resolution. However, modern JavaScript engines heavily optimize call() and apply() with inline caching. The performance difference is negligible for most applications. The main cost is when call() is used in hot loops or with very large argument lists.

codeExamples.title: Basic call() Usage
codeExamples.description: call() lets us pass different 'this' values to the same function. The function can be used with any object that has a 'name' property.

codeExamples.title: Method Borrowing: Array Methods on Array-Like Objects
codeExamples.description: call() enables method borrowing. Array.prototype methods work on any array-like object with length and numeric indices.

codeExamples.title: Constructor Chaining with call()
codeExamples.description: Animal.call(this, 'mammal') runs the Animal constructor on the new Dog instance, setting up inherited properties. This is constructor stealing.

codeExamples.title: Reliable Type Detection with toString.call()
codeExamples.description: Object.prototype.toString.call() returns precise type strings like '[object Array]'. This is more accurate than typeof for arrays, null, and other objects.

codeExamples.title: call() with Math Methods on Custom Objects
codeExamples.description: call() works with any function, including built-ins. You can borrow methods like push or Math.max to work with custom objects. However, modern ES6+ features often provide cleaner alternatives.

mcqQuestions.question: What does call() do?
options: Creates a new function bound to this
Immediately invokes a function with specified this and comma-separated args
mcqQuestions.explanation: call() immediately invokes the function with a specified 'this' value and arguments passed as a comma-separated list.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between call() and apply()?
options: call() takes arguments individually, apply() takes an array
call() does not accept thisArg, apply() does
mcqQuestions.explanation: Both invoke the function immediately. call() takes arguments as a comma-separated list (fn.call(obj, 1, 2)), while apply() takes them as an array (fn.apply(obj, [1, 2])).

mcqQuestions.question: What will the following log? function f() { console.log(this.x); } const obj = { x: 1 }; f.call(obj);
mcqQuestions.explanation: call() sets 'this' to obj inside the function, so this.x is obj.x which is 1.

mcqQuestions.question: What does Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments) do?
options: Converts the arguments object to a real array
Returns the arguments as a string
mcqQuestions.explanation: This borrows Array.prototype.slice and calls it with the arguments object as 'this'. Since arguments is array-like, slice() converts it to a real array.

mcqQuestions.question: What happens when you pass null as thisArg to call() in non-strict mode?
mcqQuestions.explanation: In non-strict mode, null or undefined as thisArg causes 'this' to default to the global object (window in browsers).

mcqQuestions.question: How do you use call() for constructor chaining?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Parent.call(this, ...args) inside a Child constructor runs the Parent constructor on the current Child instance, setting up inherited properties on this.

mcqQuestions.question: What is method borrowing?
options: Copying all methods of an object
Using a method from one object on another via call/apply
Creating a new method on an object
Deleting a method from an object
mcqQuestions.explanation: Method borrowing is the pattern of taking a method from one object (like Array.prototype.slice) and calling it on another object (like arguments) using call() or apply().

mcqQuestions.question: What will this return? Object.prototype.toString.call([]);
mcqQuestions.explanation: Object.prototype.toString.call() returns a string in the format '[object Type]'. For arrays, it returns '[object Array]'.

mcqQuestions.question: Does call() modify the original function?
options: No, the original function is unchanged
mcqQuestions.explanation: call() does not modify the function. It only controls the 'this' value for that particular invocation.

mcqQuestions.question: How do you call a function with multiple arguments using call()?
mcqQuestions.explanation: call() requires arguments to be passed individually (comma-separated). To pass an array of arguments, use apply() or the spread operator with call().
